speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Ruben Zylverszpic. The date today is December the 8th, 1996. And I'm conducting an interview with Moses Kaufman in Melbourne, Victoria, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me your name and spell it please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Moses Kaufman. M-O-S-E-S, K-A-U-F-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth? And spell that.

speaker: interviewee
text: At birth it was Mojesz Kaufman. M-O-J-E-S-Z, K-A-U-F-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date of birth is August the 14th, 1912.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I am 84.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the city you were born in? And spell that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Radom, R-A-D-O-M, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the address of the home where you grew up as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was number 23 Nowogradzka Street, Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ooh. N-O-W-O-G-R-A-D-Z-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a house or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's apartment in a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe it please?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's about 12 flats. And we were occupying one of them, on the first floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many rooms did it have?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about, what, four rooms, a four-room flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were those rooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of rooms? It was two bedrooms, one lunch room, one dining room, and a kitchen, and a foyer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the other members of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My other members of the family? I got a sister, and my brother, and my father, and my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joel Jehuda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-O-E-L, J-E-H-U-D-A. Jehuda.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gitla, G-I-T-L-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was your oldest sibling? Who's the oldest, your brother or your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was the oldest one in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-E-L-E-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rubin, R-U-B-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: What line of work was your father involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Involved in? He was a master tanner. And he got his tannery in Radom, when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a tanner for his-- most of his working life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of his working life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go to the tannery as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, very often.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: What, the tannery, to describe? It was a colossal tannery, with all modern machines. And working approximately, about 30, between 30 and 35 people, working in the tannery. And that what I could tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the people who were employed by your father, were they Jewish or non-Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly non-Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now about your life at school. What school did you attend in Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: I attend [NON-ENGLISH]-- gymnasium. I finished gymnasium [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is-- how to in English-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] friends of learning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the school for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: The school was in Radom. The street was Kilinskiego Number 30.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-I-L-I-N-S-K-G-O, Kilinskiego, in Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who attended that school? Was it for Jewish and non-Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people only. It was a gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know of the number of students that were in the school when you went to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the school, that was about around 400, 450 students.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did the classes consist of? What did you learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Humanistic. Not-- that was mostly humanistic. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I learned-- that was a humanistic. That was humanistic learning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- was there Jewish study involved as well in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school? Yes. Hebrew. And also, a little bit of history. The Jewish history.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of your teacher at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Elchan Szuotzer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-L-C-H-A-N, S-Z-U-O-T-Z-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said this was a Jewish school. Who formed the school? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: The school was formed-- that was, actually, in the beginning, [NON-ENGLISH], a private school. And after the Jewish-- how do you say it-- committee of Jewish people that were the organizers, and they were taking over, that was a Jewish private school, private gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was the school from your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't far. Because Radom wasn't such a big town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the Jewish life in Radom, when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was growing up, Radom, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was 100,000 population, about 34,000 Jews were living in that town. And the town, that was the center of tanners in Poland. There was a lot of tanneries there. And most of the tanneries, they belonged to the Jewish people. Some through some volkdeutsche. And that was a very nice little town, as I remember. And that what I can tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in any Jewish cultural events, you or your family, when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, in culture events?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, were you involved in any youth groups yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, youth groups, yes. I was belonging to a Revisionist group. They called it them Masada. And I was one of the-- when this established was it--

speaker: interviewer
text: The founding

speaker: interviewee
text: When the founding was then, that I was on one of the first one.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 15 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the main aim of the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zionism. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did you attend meetings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Twice weekly, three times weekly, we were coming to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were the meetings held?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got a local in Rwanska, street in Radom. We were coming to them together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-W-A-N-S-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people would attend these meetings?

speaker: interviewee
text: The meeting's about 20, 25, sometimes 30 people. Mostly people learning at school and students, young students.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was discussed at these meetings? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Discussed was the history of Jews and also the new trend of Zionism, the new method of Zionism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father or mother involved at all in the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, especially.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was working till the war broke out. The last years, he was the president of the Zionist organization in Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a special name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zionist Organization in Radom. And he was the president of the Zionist Organization in Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you-- did you ever attend the meetings that he went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was too young. And after, that-- before the war, the years before the war, I was more engaged in my work as a tanner, master tanner.

speaker: interviewer
text: With respect to your father's job as the president of the Zionist organization, did he speak at home about his ideals and his thoughts?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he speak about?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot. A lot. My father implanted in us, in the children, the love for Zion. And he was one of the first one. I start to, in 1919, when just Zionism was born, and he was working as one of the first in Radom, especially. And he was working for that ideology.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have any plans that you know of to migrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was-- yes. But unfortunately, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he didn't-- we went through a lot of wrong things during the time-- till the war. Because I lost my mother-- the wife of my father-- really young. And I lost, a year later, I lost my brother, Rubin. And that was a hold up a little bit. But he was-- he was a very serious thinking about to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your mother and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, she was a sick person. And she passed away in 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother Rubin?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '31, a year later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his cause of death?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- he was-- one of his friends [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was sick with, I believe, typhus. He visit him. And he-- just-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he caught, of him, that sickness. And he was alive, that friend. And my brother, after, two or three weeks sickness, passed away. From that sickness, typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stick to your upbringing as a child in Radom. Do you remember attending synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, and during the holidays, mostly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the synagogue to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- the synagogue, that was next to the Zionist organization. That was in Zeromske 23, number 23.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Z-E-R-O-M-S-K-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that near the center of town?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the center of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a large synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't a large synagogue. Most of the members of the Zionist organizations were praying there during the holidays. There were holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the name of the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: This wasn't a rabbi there. No. It wasn't a rabbi. There was only a cantor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Przeyslow, Przeyslow, he was the cantor there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very hard. P-R-Z-E-Y-S-L-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you attend synagogue with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my father and my mother. And with my brother, when he was alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: At home, did you celebrate the sabbath?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother was always lighting the candles. And that was a Friday night, Saturday. That was, for us, a holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have many family, extended family members, living in Radom with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Sisters, of my mother. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And one brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the family get together at the holiday, in the holiday seasons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was a must, that all family came together on holidays, especially on holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the Passover evening?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Passover, usually that was every family was doing. It was holidaying by themselves. It's a normal thing that was happening. So, as it's happening now.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were at school, actually, when you were outside of school, did you ever experience any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot. And especially the-- the years, '30, '31, '32, '33, up to the end. Or up to the start of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your earliest recollections of anti-Semitism? At what age were you when you first experienced it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very young. I was 17, 18 years when that start.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way did it start?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a usual pattern, beating Jews, throwing stones, [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] slogans, not to buy by Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this, when you first noticed?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you doing at this stage in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my life?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't give in. I was always tough. And I didn't frighten. I didn't-- was frightened for the things what is happening, or the hooligans, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever experience, personally, any verbal or physical attacks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the perpetrators of these?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Poles, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Nationalists. They were anti-Semitic, especially after 1933, when the [NON-ENGLISH] in Germany. And they were very close-- when Hitler came to power, and Poland start to get the worst.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prior to that, how would you describe the relationship between the Jews and non-Jews of Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a lot of good friends too, Poles. Same thing applies to my mother-- to my father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- but the times I am not remembering, so-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that's for me, a very bad experience, was that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because that anti-Semitism was-- was growing and growing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you notice a change when Hitler came to power in 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And year, two years later, I noticed this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing at this time with your career? What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was already working as a tanner and consulting other firms in Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you finished gymnasium--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other form of education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have. Because actually, the technical, the practical part of my knowledge I got from my father. But I've done for one and a half years, that was Szkola Garbarska, in Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-Z-K-O-L-A. G-A-R-B-A-R-S-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you look at that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly the theoretical part, in chemistry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were saying, in 1933, you were already working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I was working in 1933. I was already working. And was cutting on some work in some tanneries in Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after that, in the next two or three years?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time, working. And consulting. Tanneries, and Lublin, and Radom. The last one, that was in Lublin, that I consulted.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first place you went to after you left your hometown of Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Lodz. My father, was-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] he got a tannery. He was renting a tannery in Radom. That was on Zgeierska, on that, 4.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Z-G-E-I-E-R-S-K-A, Zgeierska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the tannery?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. And the name of the tanner, [NON-ENGLISH]. What the father got, that was [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH], K-- J-- Jehuda, K-- Kaufman. [NON-ENGLISH], J-- K.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your father's tannery in which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lodz. And I was carrying them all. Because my father got another tannery in Radom, with partners.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of that tannery?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-K-D-A, DKDA. They were three partners. That was Den, D-E-N, K-A-U-F-M-A-N, that was Kaufman. And Danzinger. D-A-N-Z-I-N-G-E-R, Danziger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your position in this tannery in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was carrying on as a technical manager and as a director of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the tannery there was some quarters. In front of the tannery, there were some quarters. And I was living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What length of time were you living in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was life in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was quite all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. I was too busy in my tannery. Because that was lying on my back, on my shoulders, still work to carry, buying, selling, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this was, you said, in the years-- what years was it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience similar acts of anti-Semitism in Lodz that you had experienced?

speaker: interviewer
text: Exactly. Exactly, all Poland, I hate to say, that the anti-Semitism was very high. And were you in touch with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was always in touch with my family. And my father was always coming to visit me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after that, after you left Lodz? After we left Lodz, I was carrying on a tannery in Lublin, [NON-ENGLISH] number 5. The tannery belongs to Zorkarat, a gentleman by the name Zorkarat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zorkarat, Z-O-R-K-A-R-A-T. Zorkarat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you go from Lodz to Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the tannery, my father closed the firm, closed the tannery. And I went to work for Mr. Zorkarat, in Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you spend in Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was close until the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what the leather that you were working on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly sole leather. Sole leather. Sole, sole leathers.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know if it was at all for military use?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some had been for the military use.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as Hitler's rise to power continued, and war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: After war broke out, what were your thoughts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Knowing what happens to the Jews when [NON-ENGLISH] was in Germany, after 1933-- I was very worrying, what the time will bring, especially for Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lublin, I left two months before the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you leave Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went for my holidays, to Krinica.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you on the 1st of September, 1939, when war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Radom with my father when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: To describe this, I said when the war started. Is that correct? I remember the whole town was bombarded. There was people-- we were frightened. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And life was miserable.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was talking to my father. Because young people know what to do. And first thing what I done, I went to the-- I was trying-- because I was in the reserve, actually, not contingent, they are calling this. And I went to the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that was the [NON-ENGLISH], commander. Commander of [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was trying to go to the army, and to fight the Germans with a lot of my friends, school mates. And the answer was when we will need you, we will notify you. And that was the answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just going to take a break there and change tapes.
